meta.description: Slot-based sentence patterns for Lea Faka-Tonga interactive builder
meta.note: s32 (Social Formulas) dropped from builder — moved to flip cards

patterns.title: Simple Sentence — Tense + Pronoun + Verb
patterns.label_en: I eat. / She sang. / We will sleep.

assembly_rules.notes: Short pronoun follows tense marker directly. Questions formed by rising intonation with 2nd-person pronouns.

patterns.title: With Modifier — How You Did It
patterns.label_en: I worked hard. / They ate a lot. / I am very tired.

assembly_rules.notes: Modifier follows verb directly. ʻaupito (very) always goes last; can combine: lahi ʻaupito.

patterns.title: With Object — What You Did
patterns.label_en: I ate bread. / I want some taro. / Did you eat the fish?

assembly_rules.notes: Three object types: bare noun (indefinite compound), ha + noun (indefinite with article), ʻa e + noun (definite — the focus marker ʻa is never dropped before a definite object).

examples.tongan: Naʻá ke kai ʻa e ika?

patterns.title: With Time Word — When It Happened
patterns.label_en: I ate fish last night. / She will go tomorrow.

assembly_rules.notes: Time word always goes last. Object slot is optional. Past time words pair with naʻa; future time words pair with te.

examples.tongan: ʻOku ou kai mā ʻaho ni.

patterns.title: Being Somewhere — I am at home
patterns.label_en: I am at home. / Where were you last night?

assembly_rules.notes: Location as predicate — no action verb. ʻi + place implies 'to be at.' Demonstratives heni/hena/hē can fill the place slot.

patterns.title: Action at a Place — I live in Tonga
patterns.label_en: I live in Tonga. / I went to town. / I came from Fiji.

assembly_rules.notes: Preposition dropdown: ʻi (in/at), ki (to/toward), mei (from). Preposition choice interacts with verb semantics.

patterns.title: With Someone — I went with Sione
patterns.label_en: I went with Sione. / She came with Mele.

assembly_rules.notes: mo = with (accompaniment). Companion slot uses proper names.

patterns.title: Singular Command — Eat!
patterns.label_en: Eat! / Please come! / Go — you!

assembly_rules.notes: No tense marker, no pronoun. Bare verb = command to one person. muʻa (please) is optional postposed polite particle.

patterns.title: Plural Command — Go, all of you!
patterns.label_en: Eat, all of you! / Go, all of you!

assembly_rules.notes: Command to 3+ people. Plural verb forms: ʻalu becomes ō (go), haʻu becomes ō mai (come). Dual variant (Ch 10): Mo + verb commands exactly two people (Mo hopo! = Jump, you two!). Prohibitions for two: ʻoua té mo + verb.

patterns.title: Prohibition — Don't eat!
patterns.label_en: Don't eat! / Don't sleep, all of you!

assembly_rules.notes: Pronoun set: ke for singular, mou for plural. No tense variation. Verbs are lowercase (not sentence-initial).

patterns.title: Suggestion — Let's eat
patterns.label_en: Let's eat. / Shall we sing? / Shall we two go?

assembly_rules.notes: tau (inclusive plural: let's, all of us) and ta (inclusive dual: let's, us two) without a tense marker = suggestion.

patterns.title: Verbal Negation — I am not hungry
patterns.label_en: I am not angry. / They did not eat. / I will not go.

assembly_rules.notes: TM dropdown: ʻOku (present), Naʻe (past), ʻE (future). 1sg is always u in negation regardless of TM. Negation uses naʻe (not naʻa) for past and ʻe (not te) for future.

patterns.title: Ko Negation — I am not a teacher
patterns.label_en: I am not a teacher. / He is not a farmer.
patterns.label_to: ʻoku ʻikai ko e + Noun + Subject

assembly_rules.notes: Negates ko equational sentences. Fixed prefix ʻoku ʻikai ko e. Subject slot: demonstrative or postposed pronoun.

examples.tongan: ʻOku ʻikai ko e hele ʻeni.
ʻOku ʻikai ko e faiako ia.

patterns.title: Verbal Questions — Where? When? How?
patterns.label_en: Where did you sleep? / When will you go? / How are you?

assembly_rules.notes: Question word follows verb. ʻanefē pairs with past TMs only; ʻafē pairs with future TMs only. Location question words work with any tense.

patterns.title: Who? — ko hai
patterns.label_en: Who is coming? / Who sang?

assembly_rules.notes: Ko-initial question. 3sg pronoun ne typically omitted. TM dropdown: ʻoku (present), naʻe (past), ʻe (future).

patterns.title: What is that? — ko e hā
patterns.label_en: What is that? / What is this?

assembly_rules.notes: Fixed ko e hā with variable demonstrative. Answer pattern uses s18 (Ko identification).

patterns.title: Where is...? — ko fē
patterns.label_en: Where is Sione? / Where's your hat?

assembly_rules.notes: Two sub-patterns: ko fē + ʻa + Name (Where is Sione?) and ko fē + Possessive + Noun (Where's my hat?).

patterns.title: Identifying Things — This is a book
patterns.label_en: This is a book. / That is a mat.

assembly_rules.notes: The simplest ko pattern. No tense marker, no verb. Demonstrative dropdown: ʻeni (this), ʻena (that near you), ē (that over there).

patterns.title: I am a... — Equational with Pronoun
patterns.label_en: I am a teacher. / Are you a farmer?
patterns.label_to: ko e + Predicate Noun + Postposed Pronoun

assembly_rules.notes: Ko equational with pronoun subject: no ʻa before postposed pronouns. Postposed pronouns: au (I), koe (you), ia (he/she). Questions by rising intonation only.

patterns.title: He/She is a... — Equational with Name
patterns.label_en: Taniela is a teacher. / Samiu is a smart boy.
patterns.label_to: ko e + Predicate Noun + ʻa + Name

assembly_rules.notes: ʻa before proper noun subjects. Parallel to verbal noun-subject sentences (s22). Optional adjective can appear between predicate_noun and focus_marker: Ko e tamasiʻi poto ʻa Lātū.

examples.tongan: Ko e tamasiʻi poto ʻa Lātū.
Ko e tangata ngāue ʻa Sēmisi.

patterns.title: What Are You Doing? — ko + Possessive + Action
patterns.label_en: Are you hoeing? / What are you doing?
patterns.label_to: ko + Possessive + Verbal Noun (+ Prep + Place)

assembly_rules.notes: ko + ʻe-class possessive + verbal noun = present action. ʻE-class only for actions. With ko, the action is happening right now. Optional preposition + place for motion verbals (Ko ʻeku ʻalu ki Nukuʻalofa). Questions by rising intonation.

patterns.title: Named Subject — Sione went
patterns.label_en: Sione went. / Mele is happy. / Pita is sick.

assembly_rules.notes: Intransitive noun-subject pattern. Word order: TM + Verb + ʻa + Subject. Always naʻe (not naʻa) and ʻe (not te) with noun subjects. TM: Naʻe, ʻOku, ʻE, Kuo.

patterns.title: Who Did What — Transitive Sentence
patterns.label_en: Sione ate the bread. / Pita took Mele.
patterns.label_to: TM + Verb + ʻa + Object + ʻe + Agent

assembly_rules.notes: Full transitive pattern. ʻa marks the postposed object: ʻa + name for proper nouns, ʻa e + noun for definite common nouns — ʻa is never dropped before a definite object. ʻe marks the agent: ʻe + name for proper nouns, ʻe he + noun for common nouns. TMs: naʻe/ʻe/ʻoku/kuo with noun subjects (never naʻa or te). Passive variant: omit agent phrase entirely.

examples.tongan: Naʻe lau ʻa e tohí ʻe Sione.
Naʻe ʻave ʻe Pita ʻa Mele.
Naʻe kai ʻe he fefiné ʻa e iká.

patterns.title: Experiencer Verbs — I understand
patterns.label_en: I understand. / I forgot. / I love you.

assembly_rules.notes: Experiencer verbs: mahino + kiate (understand), ngalo + ʻiate (forget). TM: ʻOku, Naʻe. prep_pronoun depends on verb choice.

patterns.title: ʻE-Class — my knife (things you control)
patterns.label_en: my knife / your book / their food

assembly_rules.notes: ʻE-class possessives for controlled/consumable items: food, tools, work, animals, money. Possessive pronoun precedes the noun directly.

patterns.title: Ho-Class — my house (things that characterize you)
patterns.label_en: my house / your name / his clothes

assembly_rules.notes: Ho-class possessives for shelter/characterization items: house, name, clothes, body parts, relations, country. Possessive pronoun precedes the noun directly.

patterns.title: Of-Phrase — Sione's boat
patterns.label_en: Sione's boat. / The teacher's book.

assembly_rules.notes: Postposed possessive: ʻo (ho-class) or ʻa (ʻe-class) + possessor name. Class selection depends on the noun: ʻa for tools/food/work/animals, ʻo for house/body/clothes/shelter. E.g. hele ʻa Sione (knife, ʻe-class), fale ʻo Sione (house, ho-class).

patterns.title: Counting Things — six baskets
patterns.label_en: six baskets / three books

assembly_rules.notes: Attributive numeral pattern: noun + ʻe + number. This ʻe is distinct from the agent marker and future tense marker. Often preceded by ha (indefinite): ha kato ʻe ono (six baskets).

patterns.title: Counting People — five people
patterns.label_en: five people / only three

assembly_rules.notes: toko replaces ʻe when counting people. No ʻe between toko and the number. tokolahi = many (people), tokosiʻi = few (people).

patterns.title: Prices — How much is the shirt?
patterns.label_en: How much is the shirt?

assembly_rules.notes: Price question pattern. Nouns take definitive accent. Answer: paʻanga ʻe + number, seniti ʻe + number.

patterns.title: Telling Time — It is three o'clock
patterns.label_en: It is three o'clock. / What time is it?

assembly_rules.notes: Time-telling uses the ko pattern. Hours take definitive accent: tolú (3), valú (8), etc. Question form: Ko e fihá eni? (What time is it?). Minutes past/to use ʻosi/toe as advanced variants.

patterns.title: Wanting and Ability — I want to eat
patterns.label_en: I want to eat. / I can go. / I like fishing.

assembly_rules.notes: Auxiliary verbs slot between pronoun and main verb. fie (want) takes verb directly: fie kai = want to eat. lava ʻo (can) inserts ʻo before the main verb: lava ʻo ʻalu = can go. saiʻia (like) takes he + definite noun rather than a verb — note as variant, not included in builder dropdown.

examples.tongan: Té ke lava ʻo haʻu ʻanai?
examples.english: Will you be able to come later?
I wanted to sleep last night.

patterns.title: Still / Already — I am still eating
patterns.label_en: I am still eating. / I have already finished. / I am working right now.

assembly_rules.notes: Aspect markers slot between pronoun and main verb (same position as auxiliaries). kei (still) pairs well with pē. ʻosi (already) pairs naturally with kuo. lolotonga (currently) marks action in progress. toe (again) indicates repetition. toki (just now/not until) marks timing. Post-verbal variant: leva (immediately) goes after the verb — note as variant.

examples.tongan: ʻOku ou lolotonga lau ha tohi.
examples.english: I am currently reading a book.

patterns.title: Should / Must — You should eat
patterns.label_en: You should eat. / You must go.
patterns.label_to: Modal + ke + (Pronoun | ʻa + Noun) + Verb (+ Prep + Place)

assembly_rules.notes: Modal obligation frames wrap around a ke-subordinate clause. ʻoku totonu (should) expresses moral obligation or probability. kuo pau (must) expresses necessity or certainty. 1sg after ke is always u (not ou/ku). Fill EITHER pronoun OR noun_subject (ʻa + name), not both. Optional preposition + place for motion verbs. Negative variant: ʻoku ʻikai totonu ke. Emphatic negative: ʻoku totonu ke ʻoua naʻa — note as advanced variant.

examples.tongan: Kuo pau ke u foki ki ʻapi.
ʻOku totonu ke tokoni ʻa Sione.

patterns.title: Comparison — Tonga is smaller than Fiji
patterns.label_en: Tēvita is stronger than Sēmisi. / The truck is faster than the bicycle.
patterns.label_to: TM + Adj + ange + ʻa + Subject + ʻi + NP

assembly_rules.notes: Tongan word order: TM + Adj + ange + ʻa + Subject + ʻi + ComparisonNP. Subject slot includes ʻa before proper nouns and ʻa e before common nouns (focus marker is locked but proper noun subjects in comparison_subjects omit the article). ʻi becomes ʻia before proper nouns in some dialects; both ʻi and ʻia are accepted. Before common nouns: ʻi he + noun. Superlative variant: replace ange with taha, drop ʻi phrase. Comparison modifiers kiʻi (a little), fuʻu (much), toe (still) go before the adjective.

examples.tongan: ʻOku mālohi ange ʻa Tēvita ʻi Sēmisi.
ʻOku vave ange ʻa e lolí ʻi he pasikalá.
examples.english: The truck is faster than the bicycle.

examples.tongan: ʻOku lelei ange ʻa Sione ʻi Mele.

patterns.title: Directional Particles — Come here
patterns.label_en: She is coming. / I will tell you. / They went down to the sea.
patterns.label_to: TM + Pronoun + Verb + Directional (+ Prep + Place)

assembly_rules.notes: Directional particle immediately follows the verb, before any objects or prepositional phrases. mai (toward speaker, 1st person), atu (away from speaker / toward listener, 2nd person), ange (toward third party, 3rd person), hake (upward / to completion), hifo (downward / to quietness), holo (around / in various directions). Note: ange also serves as comparative marker in s36 — context disambiguates. haʻu does not combine with directional particles (motion toward the speaker is already built into the verb); other motion verbs pair freely with the directionals. Commands without TM/pronoun also work: Haʻu! ʻAlu atu!

examples.tongan: Naʻa nau ō hifo ki tahi.
examples.english: They went down to the sea.

patterns.title: There Is — Existential Sentences
patterns.label_en: There is a man here. / There is no food in the house.
patterns.label_to: ʻOku ʻi ai + ha + Noun (+ Location)

assembly_rules.notes: Existential 'there is/are.' ha (indefinite) is required — you can only assert the existence of something indefinite. Location is optional but common. The classifier fo'i can appear before the noun to single out one item (ha fo'i moli = an orange). Negative variant: 'Oku 'ikai ke 'i ai ha... Other tenses: Na'e 'i ai (there was), 'E 'i ai (there will be).

examples.tongan: ʻOku ʻi ai ha tangata ʻi heni.
ʻOku ʻi ai ha foʻi moli ʻi fale.
examples.english: There is an orange in the house.

examples.tongan: ʻOku ʻi ai ha vaka ʻi tahi.
examples.english: There is a boat at sea.

patterns.title: Cleft Sentence — It was Sione who ate the bread
patterns.label_en: It was Sione who ate the bread. / It is Mele who is singing.

assembly_rules.cleft_pronoun_insertion: When verb is transitive and object is present, auto-insert pronoun between TM and verb. For 3sg proper-noun subjects: insert ne, accent TM (naʻe → naʻá). For 1sg subject (au): insert ku (naʻá ku). For 3pl subject (kinautolu / e fānau): insert nau (naʻa nau). Intransitive verbs with no object: no pronoun inserted.
assembly_rules.notes: Cleft sentences front the subject with ko for emphasis: ‘It was X who did Y.’ Transitive pattern: Ko + Subject + TM(accented) + pronoun + Verb + Object. Intransitive pattern: Ko + Subject + TM + Verb (+ Location). TMs: naʻe (past), ʻoku (present).

examples.tongan: Ko Tēvita naʻá ne kai e foʻi niú.
examples.english: It was Tēvita who ate the coconut.

examples.tongan: Ko Mele naʻá ne fufulu ʻa e valá.
examples.english: It was Mele who washed the clothes.
It is Pita who works here.
It was the children who sang.

patterns.title: Noun Classes — to Mele, to the teacher
patterns.label_en: I spoke to Mele. / He helped the teacher. / We sailed from Haʻapai.

assembly_rules.notes: Ch 46 noun classes: the preposition form is governed by the noun class of the place. Personal nouns take kia/ʻia/meia (kia Mele), local nouns take ki/ʻi/mei (ki Vavaʻu), common nouns take ki he/ʻi he/mei he (ki he faiakó). The engine auto-pairs the place to the matching preposition form.

examples.english: We will sail from Haʻapai. (local: mei)

patterns.title: Describing Character — I am eager
patterns.label_en: I am upset. / She is courageous. / They are kind.

assembly_rules.notes: Ch 35: compound adjectives (loto- emotional states, anga-/sino- character compounds) sit in the verb slot with a tense marker, exactly like any Tongan adjective. 'ʻOku ou loto mamahi' = 'I am upset'.

patterns.title: When It Happened — months and time
patterns.label_en: I came in January. / He will return in November.

assembly_rules.notes: Ch 42: month names take a bare ʻi with no article (ʻi Sānuali = in January, never ʻi he Sānuali). The time phrase goes last.
